sections.title: Be careful
items: To form a question, invert the modal and the subject: **Should** we leave now?
Modals can also be used in passive structures: The form **must be signed** by a parent.

sections.title: Be careful
items: Use **be able to** when you need a tense that *can* does not have: It's great to **be able to** work from home.
Future: After more practice, you'll **be able to** speak Spanish fluently.
Present perfect: Have you **been able to** reach her on the phone?

sections.title: Be careful
items: **May** sounds more formal and polite than **could**, and **could** is more polite than **can**.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Complete with can, could or be able to
exercises.title: A: Complete with can, could or be able to
exercises.instructions: Write the correct form in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: I enjoy swimming, but I ...... dive very well yet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not able yet, so the correct answer is "can't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: When my dad was young, he ...... climb trees easily.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past ability, so the correct answer is "could".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modal verbs express different degrees of possibility, ability, and permission.

items.sentence: After a few more lessons, she'll ...... ride a motorbike safely.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is future ability, so the correct answer is "be able to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "be able to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: They haven't ...... contact the hotel yet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present perfect ability, so the correct answer is "been able to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: ...... you speak English when you were ten?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past ability question, so the correct answer is "Could".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: If we save enough money, we'll ...... buy a better computer.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is future with condition, so the correct answer is "be able to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "be able to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: I'm sorry, I ...... meet you after school today.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not possible today, so the correct answer is "can't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: He wasn't ...... open the door because he had lost the key.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past inability, so the correct answer is "able to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "able to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: B: Permission (choose the correct modal)
exercises.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct word or phrase.

items.sentenceAfter: I speak to the manager, please?
items.originalSentence: **Can / May** I speak to the manager, please?
items.completedSentence: May I speak to the manager, please?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Ability or informal permission fits better.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: May is formal permission. Can is everyday permission or ability.

wrongOptions.can: Can can sound informal where a rule or polite permission is needed.
wrongOptions.may: May is formal — it may not fit informal ability sentences.
wrongOptions.Can: "Can" breaks the grammar pattern described above.

items.sentenceAfter: use my charger if your phone battery is low.
items.originalSentence: You **can / could** use my charger if your phone battery is low.
items.completedSentence: You can use my charger if your phone battery is low.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The speaker is giving permission now.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Can often gives permission or states ability. Could is more polite or past.

wrongOptions.can: Can may be too direct or present-tense for this context.
wrongOptions.could: Could may be too tentative when clear present permission is meant.

items.sentenceAfter: you help me carry this box, please?
items.originalSentence: **Could / Can** you help me carry this box, please?
items.completedSentence: Could you help me carry this box, please?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Could softens the request or refers to past ability.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Can often gives permission or states ability. Could is more polite or past.

wrongOptions.can: Can may be too direct or present-tense for this context.
wrongOptions.could: Could may be too tentative when clear present permission is meant.
wrongOptions.Can: "Can" breaks the grammar pattern described above.

items.originalSentence: Visitors **may / could** enter the museum after 10 a.m.
items.completedSentence: Visitors may enter the museum after 10 a.m.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The level of formality and meaning in the sentence matches one modal, not the other.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: May states permission or possibility in formal English. Could is a polite request or past ability.

wrongOptions.could: Could does not express the same type of permission or rule here.
wrongOptions.may: May does not match the intended meaning or register here.

items.sentenceAfter: I have another copy of the form?
items.originalSentence: **May / Can** I have another copy of the form?
items.completedSentence: May I have another copy of the form?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Ability or informal permission fits better.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: May is formal permission. Can is everyday permission or ability.

wrongOptions.can: Can can sound informal where a rule or polite permission is needed.
wrongOptions.may: May is formal — it may not fit informal ability sentences.
wrongOptions.Can: "Can" breaks the grammar pattern described above.

items.originalSentence: You **couldn't / can't** swim here. It's dangerous.
items.completedSentence: You can't swim here. It's dangerous.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "can't" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "can't" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "couldn't" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.couldn't: "couldn't" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("swim here. It's dangerous.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: you show me the way to the library?
items.originalSentence: Excuse me, **could / may** you show me the way to the library?
items.completedSentence: Excuse me, could you show me the way to the library?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The level of formality and meaning in the sentence matches one modal, not the other.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: May states permission or possibility in formal English. Could is a polite request or past ability.

wrongOptions.could: Could does not express the same type of permission or rule here.
wrongOptions.may: May does not match the intended meaning or register here.

items.originalSentence: No, you **may not / could not** take photographs inside the gallery.
items.completedSentence: No, you may not take photographs inside the gallery.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "may not" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "may not" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "could not" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.could not: "could not" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("take photographs inside the gallery.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: C: Advice (should / ought to)
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with should, shouldn't, ought to or ought not to.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with should, shouldn't, ought to or ought not to.

items.sentence: It's raining. You ...... take an umbrella.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is good advice, so the correct answer is "should".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: We ...... leave our bags unattended at the station.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is bad idea, so the correct answer is "shouldn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: If she wants to improve, she ...... practise every day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is advice, so the correct answer is "ought to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: You ...... spend all afternoon looking at a screen.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is bad idea, so the correct answer is "ought not to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "ought not to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: What ...... we do if the train is cancelled?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is asking for advice, so the correct answer is "should".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: You ...... ask your parents before you go out.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is advice, so the correct answer is "should".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: Drivers ...... use their phones while driving.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is bad idea, so the correct answer is "shouldn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: We ...... check the address before we leave.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is advice, so the correct answer is "ought to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "ought to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

exercises.title: D: Rewrite using a modal
exercises.instructions: Rewrite each sentence using the modal in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Rewrite each sentence using the modal in brackets.

items.sentence:
Is it possible for you to send me the file today? (can)
...... you send me the file today?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is ability/possibility, so the correct answer is "Can".

wrongOptions.can: The bare infinitive (can) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
It is not a good idea to eat too quickly. (shouldn't)
You ...... eat too quickly.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is bad idea, so the correct answer is "shouldn't".

wrongOptions.shouldn't: The bare infinitive (shouldn't) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence:
I had the ability to play chess when I was six. (could)
I ...... play chess when I was six.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past ability, so the correct answer is "could".

wrongOptions.could: The bare infinitive (could) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence:
Is it all right if I open the door? (may)
...... I open the door?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is permission, so the correct answer is "May".

wrongOptions.may: The bare infinitive (may) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence:
It is not a good idea to leave your bike unlocked. (shouldn't)
You ...... leave your bike unlocked.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is bad idea, so the correct answer is "shouldn't".

wrongOptions.shouldn't: The bare infinitive (shouldn't) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence:
They have managed to repair the machine. (been able to)
They have ...... repair the machine.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is managed to, so the correct answer is "been able to".

wrongOptions.been able to: The bare infinitive (been able to) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (been able to) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

exercises.title: E: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using a modal verb so that it means the same as the first.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using a modal verb so that it means the same as the first.

items.sentence:
It's a bad idea to walk home alone late at night.
You ...... home alone late at night.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is bad idea, so the correct answer is "shouldn't walk".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "shouldn't walk", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence:
Is it OK if I borrow your bike?
...... your bike?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is permission, so the correct answer is "May I borrow".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "May I borrow", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
When I was nine, I had the ability to cook simple meals.
When I was nine, I ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past ability, so the correct answer is "could cook".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "could cook", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modal verbs express different degrees of possibility, ability, and permission.

items.sentence:
Soon they will manage to finish the bridge.
Soon they will ...... finish the bridge.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is future ability, so the correct answer is "be able to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "be able to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Do you think I should apologise to her?
Do you think I ...... to her?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is advice, so the correct answer is "ought to apologise".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "ought to apologise", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence:
Please don't enter this room without permission.
You ...... this room without permission.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is prohibition, so the correct answer is "mustn't enter".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "mustn't enter", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: 'I feel really nervous about the interview.' 'You

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "shouldn't worry" — reassurance.

wrongOptions.A: "should worry" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "shouldn't worry" is correct.
wrongOptions.should worry: "should worry" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "shouldn't worry" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "may worry" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "shouldn't worry" is correct.
wrongOptions.may worry: "may worry" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "shouldn't worry" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "can't worry" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "shouldn't worry" is correct.
wrongOptions.can't worry: "can't worry" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "shouldn't worry" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "could" — past ability.

wrongOptions.A: "can" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could" is correct.
wrongOptions.can: "can" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "am able to" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could" is correct.
wrongOptions.am able to: "am able to" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "should" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could" is correct.
wrongOptions.should: "should" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "could" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceAfter: I use your ruler?' 'Yes, of course.'

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "May" — permission request.

wrongOptions.B: "Should" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "May" is correct.
wrongOptions.Should: "Should" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "May" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "Ought" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "May" is correct.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "May" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceAfter: take a coat because it's cold outside.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "should" — advice because it's cold.

wrongOptions.B: "can" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "should" is correct.
wrongOptions.can: "can" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "should" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "could" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "should" is correct.
wrongOptions.could: "could" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "should" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "may not" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "should" is correct.
wrongOptions.may not: "may not" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "should" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Possessive forms and possessive determiners show who something belongs to.

items.sentenceBefore: By the end of the course, you

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "will be able to" — future ability.

wrongOptions.A: "could" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "will be able to" is correct.
wrongOptions.could: "could" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "will be able to" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "may" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "will be able to" is correct.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Future forms (will, going to, present continuous) depend on whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "shouldn't" — rule in a library.

wrongOptions.A: "should" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "shouldn't" is correct.

exercises.title: G: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one mistake with a modal. Write the correct sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one mistake with a modal. Write the correct sentence.

items.displayPrompt: You should to call your grandmother.

cues: You should to call your grandmother.

items.displayPrompt: May I to ask a question?

cues: May I to ask a question?

items.displayPrompt: We didn't could find the address.

cues: We didn't could find the address.

items.displayPrompt: She will can join us next week.

cues: She will can join us next week.

items.answer: She will be able to join us next week.
acceptedAnswers: She will be able to join us next week.
items.explanation: Future ability → **will be able to** (not **will can**).

items.displayPrompt: They ought not arrive so late.

cues: They ought not arrive so late.

items.answer: They ought not to arrive so late.
acceptedAnswers: They ought not to arrive so late.

exercises.title: H: Mixed practice
exercises.instructions: Complete each gap with one suitable modal expression.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each gap with one suitable modal expression.

items.sentence: Excuse me, ...... I sit next to you?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is polite permission, so the correct answer is "may".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: You ...... drink more water when it's hot.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is health advice, so the correct answer is "should".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: I ...... tie my shoes until I was five.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past inability, so the correct answer is "couldn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: With more practice, you'll ...... play this song perfectly.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is future ability, so the correct answer is "be able to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "be able to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: ...... you possibly speak more slowly, please?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is polite request, so the correct answer is "Could".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Comparatives and superlatives compare people, things, or actions.

items.sentence: We ...... book the tickets before they sell out.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is advice, so the correct answer is "ought to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "ought to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: Visitors ...... touch the paintings in the museum.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is prohibition, so the correct answer is "mustn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: My cousin ...... speak Italian quite well now.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present ability, so the correct answer is "can".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modal verbs express different degrees of possibility, ability, and permission.

exercises.title: I: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence:
I knew how to swim at the age of six.
I ...... at the age of six.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past ability, so the correct answer is "could swim".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "could swim", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
It's against the rules to take photos here.
You ...... photos here.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is against the rules, so the correct answer is "aren't allowed to take".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "aren't allowed to take", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
The dentist told me to eat fewer sweets.
The dentist said I ...... fewer sweets.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is reported advice, so the correct answer is "should eat".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "should eat", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Indirect speech keeps statement word order — do not invert subject and auxiliary.

items.sentence:
She managed to answer all the questions correctly.
She ...... all the questions correctly.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is succeeded, so the correct answer is "was able to answer".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "was able to answer", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Is it OK to use dictionaries in the test?
...... dictionaries in the test?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is permission question, so the correct answer is "Are we allowed to use".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "Are we allowed to use", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is advice, so the correct answer is "ought to train".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "ought to train", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
